era_context: The Year of the Six Emperors in the Roman Empire—a period of extreme political instability following the assassination of Severus Alexander. The Sassanid Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power. In China, the Three Kingdoms period was well established, with Cao Wei, Eastern Wu, and Shu Han competing for dominance. This was a year of crisis in Rome but relative stability in the East.

events.title: Year of the Six Emperors: Maximinus Thrax assassinated; Gordian I and II proclaimed
events.description: In early 238 CE, the unpopular emperor Maximinus Thrax was assassinated by his own troops during a siege of Aquileia. In response, the Roman Senate proclaimed the elderly Gordian I and his son Gordian II as co-emperors. This marked the beginning of the chaotic Year of the Six Emperors, during which six different men held the imperial throne. The rapid succession of emperors reflected deep instability in the empire's political system and the military's power to make and unmake rulers.
key_figures: Gordian II (Proclaimed co-emperor, son of Gordian I)

sources.name: Coins of Maximinus Thrax and Gordian I

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Maximinus Thrax and the proclamation of Gordian I and II are confirmed by numismatic evidence and multiple historical sources. The date is securely established.

events.title: Balbinus and Pupienus proclaimed emperors; Gordian I and II defeated
events.description: Following the deaths of Gordian I and II (who were defeated by the governor Capelianus in Africa), the Senate proclaimed Balbinus and Pupienus as co-emperors. These two elderly senators represented the Senate's attempt to reassert control over the imperial succession. However, their reign was brief and marked by conflict with the praetorian guard, who resented the Senate's assertion of power.
key_figures: Capelianus (Governor of Africa, defeated Gordian I and II)
events.certainty_note: The proclamation and reign of Balbinus and Pupienus are confirmed by numismatic and historical sources.

events.title: Gordian III proclaimed emperor; Balbinus and Pupienus assassinated
events.description: The praetorian guard, dissatisfied with the Senate's choice of Balbinus and Pupienus, assassinated both emperors and proclaimed the young Gordian III (grandson of Gordian I) as emperor. Gordian III, born around 225 CE, was approximately 13 years old at his accession. The praetorian prefect Timesitheus became the effective ruler, governing on behalf of the young emperor. This marked the end of the Year of the Six Emperors and the beginning of a period of relative stability under Gordian III.
events.certainty_note: Gordian III's proclamation and the assassination of Balbinus and Pupienus are confirmed by numismatic and historical sources.

events.title: Shapur I becomes King of Kings of the Sassanid Empire
events.description: Shapur I (r. 240–270 CE) succeeded his father Ardashir I as King of Kings of the Sassanid Empire. Although his reign is conventionally dated from 240 CE, the succession likely occurred in 238–239 CE. Shapur I would become one of the most powerful Sassanid rulers, conducting extensive military campaigns against Rome and consolidating Persian control over Mesopotamia and the Levant.

sources.name: Res Gestae Divi Saporis (Shapur's own inscription at Naqsh-i Rustam)

events.certainty_note: Shapur I's succession is attested in his own inscriptions, but the exact date is sometimes given as 238, 239, or 240 CE. The conventional date is 240 CE, but the succession may have occurred slightly earlier.

events.title: Cao Rui reigns as Emperor of Cao Wei
events.description: Cao Rui (r. 226–239 CE) continued his reign as emperor of Cao Wei, the dominant power in northern China. By 238 CE, Cao Rui was in the final years of his reign, which would end with his death in 239 CE. His reign was marked by military campaigns against rival kingdoms and administrative consolidation. Cao Rui was known for his cultural patronage and military acumen.
events.certainty_note: Cao Rui's reign is securely dated and well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Eastern Wu consolidates control over southern China
events.description: Under Sun Quan (r. 229–252 CE), Eastern Wu continued to consolidate its control over the Yangtze River valley and southern coastal regions. By 238 CE, Wu had established itself as a major power in the Three Kingdoms period, with a developed administrative system, naval capabilities, and economic resources. The southern kingdom was increasingly distinct from the northern Wei state in culture, economy, and governance.
events.certainty_note: Eastern Wu's political consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records and supported by archaeological evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The Historia Augusta's account of the Year of the Six Emperors contains legendary and fabricated material, particularly regarding the personal characteristics and motivations of the emperors. Modern scholarship treats it with caution. The exact sequence and dating of events within 238 CE is sometimes disputed; some sources place certain events in 237 or 239 CE. The Historia Augusta's portrayal of Timesitheus as a heroic figure is likely propagandistic. Shapur I's exact accession date (238, 239, or 240 CE) is debated among scholars, with different sources giving different dates.
historiographic_note: 238 CE is richly documented for the Roman Empire, with numismatic evidence supplementing later literary sources. However, the Historia Augusta is problematic and contains fabrications. Chinese sources are reliable for the Three Kingdoms period. Sassanid sources are limited to royal inscriptions, which present a propagandistic view. The year is essentially undocumented for most of the world outside the Mediterranean and East Asia. The Year of the Six Emperors reflects the deep instability of the Roman Empire in the third century, with the military's power to make and unmake emperors becoming increasingly apparent.

graph_edges.note: The assassination of Maximinus Thrax and proclamation of Gordian I and II led to their defeat and the proclamation of Balbinus and Pupienus
The assassination of Balbinus and Pupienus led to the proclamation of Gordian III
Gordian III's proclamation led to his consolidation of power under Timesitheus
Shapur I's accession led to his consolidation and military campaigns
Cao Rui's reign ended with his death and Cao Fang's succession
Eastern Wu's consolidation was an ongoing process throughout this period
